title: Why Data is Messy
concept: Real-world datasets are rarely clean, containing missing values, typos, and format issues that confuse machine learning models.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Bayes' Theorem is used for…?

options.text: Updating the probability of a belief as we get new evidence
Calculating the exact average of a dataset
Removing duplicate entries from a list

exercises.explain: Right! Bayes' Theorem calculates how new evidence changes our existing beliefs.

cards.title: The real world is messy
cards.body: In textbooks, datasets are perfect. In the real world, data is collected by humans and buggy software, resulting in a mess of errors.

cards.title: What is dirty data?
cards.body: Dirty data refers to datasets containing missing entries, typographical errors, inconsistent formatting, or duplicate records that break ML models.

cards.title: Baking with bad ingredients
cards.body: Imagine trying to bake a cake with spoiled milk, missing eggs, and salt instead of sugar. A machine learning model is like a recipe: garbage ingredients yield garbage results.

cards.title: A messy customer database
cards.body: In a customer list, one age is recorded as -5, another is blank, and some addresses use "NY" while others use "New York". This is dirty data.

cards.title: AI isn't doing the dirty work
cards.body: Many think data scientists spend all day training models. In reality, they spend up to 80% of their time just cleaning and preparing data!

cards.title: Raw vs Clean
cards.body: Why data cleaning is necessary before feeding it to algorithms:

exercises.prompt: Why is it dangerous to feed raw, dirty data directly into a machine learning model?

options.text: It can lead the model to learn incorrect patterns and make poor predictions.
It will make the model train too quickly.
It deletes the raw dataset from the computer.
It changes the coding language of the app.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Garbage in, garbage out. If a model learns from messy or incorrect data, its predictions will be untrustworthy.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Data cleaning is a minor task that takes up less than 5% of a data scientist's time.
exercises.explain: Correct! Data scientists actually spend up to 80% of their time cleaning and formatting data.
Yes! If you feed low-quality data into a model (garbage in), it will produce low-quality results (garbage out).

exercises.prompt: Which one is an example of dirty data?

options.text: A column of temperatures containing "N/A" and "Hot" instead of numbers
A database of heights recorded consistently in centimeters
A table of house sales where every row has a price, size, and location

exercises.explain: Correct! Mixing text descriptions like 'Hot' with missing indicators like 'N/A' in a numerical column is a classic example of dirty data.

exercises.prompt: You are training an AI to predict house prices. You notice the dataset contains some houses with negative prices and others with missing square footage. What is the best first step?

options.text: Clean the dataset by fixing or removing these errors before training
Train the model immediately, since AI automatically ignores errors
Delete all columns and start from scratch
Multiply all prices by negative one

exercises.explain: Spot on! Cleaning the data first ensures the model doesn't learn impossible patterns (like negative prices). You're ready to learn how to deal with those empty spots!
